items.q: Should testing wait until the whole program is finished?
items.correct: No - testing should happen iteratively as each module is built, so errors are found early when they are cheapest and easiest to fix.
items.misconception: Yes - testing is a single final stage after all coding is complete.
items.random_wrong: Yes - testing before completion always gives meaningless results.
items.why: The neat stage diagram suggests strict sequence. In practice development cycles: build a part, test it, refine - final testing then confirms the integrated whole.

items.q: Is analysis just starting to write the code straight away?
items.correct: No - analysis defines the problem and requirements before any code is written; coding without analysis risks building the wrong solution entirely.
items.misconception: Yes - the fastest start is to begin typing code immediately.
items.random_wrong: Yes - analysis means choosing the colour scheme first.
items.why: Eagerness to code skips the thinking. The cost of fixing a misunderstood requirement grows enormously the later it is discovered - analysis is the cheap insurance.

items.q: Are testing and evaluation the same activity?
items.correct: No - testing finds errors and checks behaviour, while evaluation judges the overall solution against requirements and criteria, including usability and efficiency.
items.misconception: Yes - once the tests pass, evaluation has automatically happened.
items.random_wrong: Yes - evaluation is just running the tests a second time.
items.why: Both come late and both judge the product, so they merge. Tests answer 'does it work?'; evaluation answers 'is it a good solution to the original problem?'

items.q: The first stage in a systematic approach to problem solving is:
items.correct: Analysis - defining the problem and its requirements
items.why: Correct answer: Analysis - defining the problem and its requirements. “Writing all the program code” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Planned algorithms, data structures and interface designs, before coding begins
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Planned algorithms, data structures and interface designs, before coding begins. “The final boxed product” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The main purpose of testing is to:
items.correct: Find errors and check the solution meets its requirements
items.misconception: Prove mathematically that no bugs can exist
items.random_wrong: Make the code run more slowly
items.why: It's Find errors and check the solution meets its requirements. “Prove mathematically that no bugs can exist” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Evaluation involves judging the solution against:
items.correct: The requirements and criteria established during analysis
items.misconception: The personal taste of one programmer
items.random_wrong: The number of lines of code written
items.why: Correct answer: The requirements and criteria established during analysis. “The personal taste of one programmer” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many stages are in the analysis, design, implementation, testing, evaluation cycle?
items.why: Exact answer: 5 stages. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
